scenario: ☕ Café English — Ordering: 'Can I get a...'
text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
At a café, the most natural way to order is not 'I would like...' but: {{c1::"Can I get a large oat milk latte, please?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə lɑːrdʒ oʊt mɪlk læ·ɾeɪ pliːz'
Rules applied:
• Can I → /kə·naɪ/ (schwa: 'can' loses its /æ/)
• get a → /gɛ·ɾə/ (Flap T cross-boundary + schwa)
• latte → /ˈlæ·ɾeɪ/ (Flap T within word)

🧠 'I would like' sounds overly formal. 'Can I get' is the default in US/Canadian café culture. 'May I have' sounds like you're in a 1950s movie.
usage:
• "Can I get a small black coffee?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə smɔːl blæk ˈkɑ·fi/
  • "Can I get that to go?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·ðæ·tuh goʊ/ (Flap T: 'that to')
  • "Can I get an extra shot?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·ɾæn·ˈɛks·trə ʃɑt/
  • "For here or to go?" — staff question → /fər·hɪr·ər·tuh·goʊ/ (all schwa)
spanish: 'Can I get a...' es la forma más natural de pedir en una cafetería en EE.UU. — no 'I would like'. Pronunciación nativa: 'kə-naɪ-gɛ-rə...' — 'can' pierde su vocal /æ/ y se convierte en /kə/ (schwa), y 'get a' se fusiona con un Flap T.

text:
🎧 YouGlish
To customize a café order, common requests are: {{c1::"Can you make that with oat milk?" / "Can I get an extra shot?" / "Can you go light on the ice?"}}
explanation:
Key phrases and native pronunciation:
• 'make that with' → /meɪk·ðæ·wɪð/ — linking R/consonant
• 'oat milk' → /ˈoʊt̚·mɪlk/ — unreleased stop: the /t/ of 'oat' is held before 'milk', no burst
• 'light on the' → /laɪ·ɾɑn·ðuh/ — Flap T: 't' of 'light' + 'on' → tap

🧠 'Light on the ice' = less ice. 'Go easy on' = use less of. 'Extra' → /ˈɛks·trə/ (schwa at end).
usage:
• "Can you make that extra hot?" → barista will heat it to a higher temp
  • "Can I get that half sweet / no whip?"
  • "Can you make it a double?" = two espresso shots
  • "Room for cream?" — barista asking if you want space left for dairy
  • "Just a splash of milk." → /splæʃ·əv·mɪlk/ (schwa: 'of' → /əv/)
spanish: Personalizar un pedido en la cafetería: 'Can you make that with oat milk?' / 'Can I get an extra shot?' / 'Can you go light on the ice?' — Nota fonética: 'oat milk' tiene una oclusiva no liberada: la /t/ de 'oat' se bloquea antes de 'milk' sin explotar.

scenario: ☕ Café English — Asking what's in something
text:
🎧 YouGlish
When curious about a menu item, ask: {{c1::"What's in this?" or "What does that come with?"}}
explanation:
Native pronunciation:
• 'What's in this?' → /wʌ·sɪn·ðɪs/ — 'What's' + 'in' → linking: the /s/ of 'what's' bridges to 'in'
• 'What does that come with?' → /wʌ·dəz·ðæ·kʌm·wɪð/ — schwa in 'does' /dəz/

🧠 Common follow-up: 'Is there any caffeine in it?' → /ɪz·ðɛr·ɛ·ni·kæ·fiːn·ɪ·nɪt/ — linking 'in it' bridges to a single unit 'ini-nit'.
usage:
• "What's in the matcha latte?" (milk + matcha powder + syrup)
  • "Does this have dairy in it?" (checking for allergies)
  • "Is the syrup included?" → /ɪz·ðuh·ˈsɪrəp·ɪŋ·ˈkluːdɪd/
  • "What's the difference between a latte and a flat white?" → common café question
  • Flat white: stronger espresso ratio, microfoam. Latte: more milk, larger.
spanish: '¿Qué tiene esto?' → 'What's in this?' /wʌ·sɪn·ðɪs/ — la /s/ de 'What's' y la /ɪ/ de 'in' forman un enlace. '¿Con qué viene eso?' → 'What does that come with?' /wʌ·dəz·ðæ·kʌm·wɪð/ — 'does' se reduce a /dəz/ (schwa).

scenario: ☕ Café English — Small talk with the barista
text:
🎧 YouGlish
The standard barista small talk exchange: Barista: "How are you doing today?" → Best native reply: {{c1::"Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?"}}
explanation:
Native speed of 'How are you doing?' → 'haʊ·ər·juh·duː·ɪŋ'
• 'are you' → /ər·juh/ — H-drop + schwa
• 'doing' → /ˈduː·ɪŋ/ (Intrusive glide: /duː-wɪŋ/)

'Pretty good' → /ˈprɪ·ɾi·ɡʊd/ — Flap T: 'pretty' /ˈprɪ·ɾi/
'Yourself?' → short for 'And how about yourself?' — very common ellipsis

🧠 'I'm fine, thank you' sounds formal/robotic. Native speakers say 'Pretty good', 'Not bad', 'Hanging in there', 'Can't complain'.
usage:
• "Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?" ← most natural
  • "Not bad! How about you?" ← slightly more engaged
  • "Can't complain! Busy morning though." ← friendly, opens dialogue
  • "Living the dream!" ← slightly ironic/humorous, works with regulars
  • "Hanging in there." ← honest, slightly tired — barista will empathize
spanish: El intercambio estándar de small talk en una cafetería. 'How are you doing?' → /haʊ·ər·juh·duː·ɪŋ/ (caída de H + schwa). Respuesta más natural: 'Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?' — 'pretty' tiene Flap T: /ˈprɪ·ɾi/. Nunca digas 'I'm fine, thank you' en una cafetería — suena robótico.

scenario: ☕ Café English — When something's wrong with your order
text:
🎧 YouGlish
When your order is wrong, the polite native way is: {{c1::"Hey, sorry to bug you — I think there might be a mistake with my order."}}
explanation:
Key phrase breakdown:
• 'sorry to bug you' — 'bug' = bother/disturb (colloquial). Much softer than 'You made a mistake.'
• 'there might be' → /ðər·maɪ·bi/ — schwa: 'there' → /ðər/, 'might' → /maɪ/, 'be' unstressed → /bi/
• 'I think' signals hedging — softens the complaint without backing down

🧠 American complaint culture is indirect: you present it as a possible error, not an accusation. This keeps the interaction positive and gets results faster.
usage:
• "Excuse me, I ordered a soy latte but this tastes like regular milk."
  • "I think this might be someone else's order."
  • "No worries at all — could you remake this for me?"
  • "My bad — I might not have said it clearly. Could I get it with oat milk?" (own the mistake even if it's theirs — very American)
spanish: Cuando algo está mal en tu pedido, el estilo nativo americano es indirecto: 'Hey, sorry to bug you — I think there might be a mistake.' No acuses directamente — presenta el problema como un posible error. 'Bug you' = molestarte. 'There might be' → /ðər·maɪ·bi/ (schwa en 'there' y 'be').

text: A 'drip coffee' is {{c1::regular brewed filter coffee — not espresso based}}. A 'cold brew' is {{c1::coffee steeped in cold water for 12-24 hours — smoother, stronger}}.
explanation:
Café vocabulary map:
• Drip/Regular coffee = basic filter brew. 'Can I get a drip?' is common in diners.
• Cold brew vs Iced coffee: cold brew is steeped cold; iced coffee is hot coffee poured over ice.
• Americano = espresso + hot water (similar strength to drip)
• Macchiato → /mæ·kiˈɑː·toʊ/ — Flap T in '-ato': /ˈɑː·ɾoʊ/
• Cortado = espresso + equal parts warm milk (Spanish origin, rising in US)

🧠 At Starbucks: 'a tall' = small, 'a grande' = medium, 'a venti' = large. Yes, it's backwards.
usage:
• "I'll take a medium drip, room for cream."
  • "What's the difference between cold brew and iced coffee?"
  • "Is the cold brew single or double concentrated?"
  • "I'll go with a cortado — I don't want too much milk."
spanish: Vocabulario clave de cafetería: Drip coffee = café filtrado normal. Cold brew = café macerado en agua fría 12-24h (más suave y fuerte). Iced coffee = café caliente sobre hielo. Americano = espresso + agua caliente. En Starbucks: tall = pequeño, grande = mediano, venti = grande (sí, está al revés).

text:
🎧 YouGlish
When paying at a café, if paying cash say: {{c1::"Keep the change."}} If on a card and tipping feels awkward: {{c1::"I'll tip on the app / I already tipped."}}
explanation:
'Keep the change' → /kiːp·ðuh·tʃeɪndʒ/
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa, unstressed)
• Linking: 'keep the' → 'keep-the' fuse — the /p/ of 'keep' attaches to 'the': /kiːp·ðuh/

US tipping culture at cafés: 15-20% is standard at table service. At counter service (Starbucks-style), tipping is optional but the screen will prompt you — 15%, 20%, 25% or custom. Saying 'no tip' is 100% acceptable and not rude at counter service.
usage:
• "Keep the change." (cash, you're being generous)
  • "Can I pay with card?" → 'Cuh-naɪ·peɪ·wɪð·kɑːrd?'
  • "Do you guys take Venmo / Apple Pay?"
  • "Split it — half on each card." (splitting a bill)
  • "Can I get a receipt?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə·rɪ·siːt/ (Flap T)
spanish: 'Keep the change' = quédate con el cambio. En EE.UU., el 15-20% de propina es estándar en servicio de mesa. En mostrador (tipo Starbucks), es opcional — la pantalla te lo pedirá pero puedes decir que no sin ningún problema.
